data.title: Dyne Therapeutics: 2026 Catalysts Point To Further Upside
data.description:
Introduction

Dyne Therapeutics (DYN) is a clinical-stage biotech firm with a ~3B market cap and 2 key clinical programs. Its pipeline focused on genetically driven muscle diseases using its proprietary FORCE™ platform. This technology uses a transferrin receptor 1 (TfR1) binding antibody fragment (fab) to deliver therapeutic payloads (ASOs, PMOs, siRNAs) directly into skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle, as well as the central nervous system (CNS).

The recent acquisition of its close competitor Avidity Biosciences by Novartis is a good validation for the rather unproven field of antibody-ASO/PMO conjugates Dyne is known for, and its share price jumped ~40% on the news. However, in this article I will explain:

1. While its first key clinical-stage drug, DYNE-101 (Zeleciment basivarsen), for DM1 is looking promising, its DMD drug, DYNE-251 (Zeleciment rostudirsen), data is weaker compared to Avidity's on their intended biomarker expression levels on a numerical basis. However, Dyne's recent release of functional data was strong, increasing the chance for Dyne to secure accelerated approval for Dyne-101.

2. With Dyne's recent close of a 350M stock offering, it is in a comfortable financial position to go it alone if it needs to, which gives them more leverage for a potential buyout. Given its "twin" company Avidity was bought at 12B last year, it makes Dyne's current valuation of 2.75B quite undervalued in comparison.

The Science

Dyne Therapeutics is focused on treating genetically driven muscle diseases using its proprietary FORCE™ platform. This technology overcomes the historic delivery limitations of muscle therapeutics by using an antibody fragment that binds to the Transferrin Receptor 1 (TfR1)-which is highly expressed on muscle cells-to deliver therapeutic payloads (ASOs, PMOs, siRNAs) directly into skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle.

The technology is similar to the famous antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) field, but instead of conjugating a small molecule to an antibody as in an ADC, they use ASO or siRNAs instead to conjugate to an antibody or a fab (which is a smaller version of an antibody). Why target muscle cells? That is because there are already FDA-approved ASOs or PMOs for muscle diseases such as DMD. In fact, Sarepta Therapeutics has 3 FDA-approved ASO therapies for DMD:

  • EXONDYS 51 (eteplirsen): Approved in September 2016 for patients amenable to exon 51 skipping, which affects about 13% of the DMD population.
  • VYONDYS 53 (golodirsen): Approved in December 2019 for patients amenable to exon 53 skipping.
  • AMONDYS 45 (casimersen): Approved in February 2021 for patients amenable to exon 45 skipping.

However, these don't work too great on their own because these drugs do not get delivered to muscle on their own, since they have no targeting moiety. As a result, their actual efficacy for treating DMD is quite terrible, with barely above baseline expression of dystrophin. In contrast, with Dyne, by conjugating the siRNAs or ASOs to a fab that targets Transferrin Receptor 1 (TfR1), a receptor commonly found in skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle, Dyne's FORCE™ platform essentially equips these drugs with a "missile" that will target the muscle cells, therefore directing these drugs to the target site to achieve a higher effective dose of these ASOs at the various muscle cells.

Pipeline at a Glance

As of late 2025, Dyne has two lead clinical programs with Breakthrough Therapy Designation from the FDA that are approaching critical regulatory milestones, alongside a growing preclinical portfolio, which we will not discuss in this article since they are too early to have significant value.

Program Indication Target Payload Development Stage Next Major Milestone
DYNE-101 Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1 (DM1) DMPK (Toxic RNA) ASO (Gapmer) Phase 1/2 ACHIEVE (Registrational Cohort) BLA Submission anticipated Q3 2027
DYNE-251 Duchenne (DMD) - Exon 51 DMD Exon 51 PMO (Exon Skipper) Phase 1/2 DELIVER (Registrational Cohort) Topline Data expected Dec 2025; BLA targeted for Q2 2026

1. DYNE-101 (Z-basivarsen)

DYNE-101's indication is Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1 (DM1). DYNE-101 is an antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) designed to reduce toxic nuclear DMPK RNA, thereby releasing spliceopathy-driving proteins (like MBNL1) to restore normal mRNA processing, and the ASO is conjugated to a fab that can target muscle as discussed earlier.

Currently it is being evaluated in a Phase 1/2 ACHIEVE Trial, and the FDA has granted Breakthrough Therapy Designation. The latest data is impressive:

a. No serious drug-related adverse events even at high doses up to 6.8 mg/kg

b. Clear functional improvement over placebo as demonstrated by tests such as vHOT (video Hand Opening Time) and functional benefits in walking and sit-to-stand tests.

Z-basivarsen led to functional improvement in DM1 patients (Dyne presentation)

However, Dyne is currently targeting a potential Accelerated Approval BLA submission for Z-basivarsen in early Q3 2027, so even if all stars are aligned, maybe it will get on the market in Q2 2028, which is quite far away for a biotech company with about 2 years of cash runway from now. In addition, it is facing competition from Avidity Biosciences with a similar drug at a similar stage. However, it is quite possible that the DM1 market is big enough for multiple players since so far there has not been any approved drug yet. More on the market analysis later.

2. DYNE-251 (Z-rostudirsen)

Indication for DYNE-251: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) amenable to exon 51 skipping. DYNE-251 is a phosphorodiamidate morpholino oligomer (PMO) conjugated to a fab that targets muscle and is designed to promote exon 51 skipping, enabling the production of a shortened but functional dystrophin protein.

Currently it is being evaluated in a Phase 1/2 DELIVER Trial, and the FDA has granted Breakthrough Therapy Designation just like DYNE-101. However, the latest data is less impressive:

a. The safety profile is a bit worrisome, as at a high dose of 40 mpk, 2 serious adverse events were seen, and this caps the dosage this drug can be used in subsequent trials:

b. For Muscle Content Adjusted Dystrophin %, which is the key biomarker for evaluating the effectiveness of the treatment, Dyne achieved 8.7% at 20 mpk vs. Avidity ~up to 25% at only 5 mpk (though 10 mpk did not seem to improve it). However, this is not as bad as it seems, because the two companies are targeting different exons. Exon 51, which is what Dyne is targeting, is arguably more difficult to target but enjoys a larger patient population, as ~13% of the DMD patient population is amenable to the therapy, compared to Exon 44 skipping therapy, which is what Avidity is doing, and it is suitable to only ~6% of the DMD patient population.

Additionally, the initially released functional data is noisier. In fact, the lower dose (10 mpk) did not seem to work, so they had to increase the dose to 20 mpk. Remember, at 40 mpk, they saw 2 serious adverse events, so that is a no-go. Therefore, the therapeutic window is quite small for Z-rostudirsen, essentially capped at a 20 mpk dose.

In December 2025, Dyne Therapeutics released promising topline functional data for zeleciment rostudirsen (DYNE-251) from its Phase 1/2 DELIVER trial. All six functional endpoints assessed showed numerical improvements in the treatment arm compared to the placebo. Notably, two of these measures-time to rise (TTR) velocity and 10-meter walk/run velocity-achieved nominal statistical significance with p-values below 0.05.

For the North Star Ambulatory Assessment (NSAA), a primary endpoint historically used in Sarepta's Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) trials, DYNE-251 demonstrated a numerical improvement of 0.2 with a p-value of 0.0894. While this specific measure did not reach the threshold for statistical significance, the trial was designed as a registrational expansion cohort to support accelerated approval and was not primarily powered for formal statistical analysis of functional outcomes.

These promising functional readouts, in addition to the fact that the primary endpoint of dystrophin expression levels was met, which demonstrated a statistically significant increase in dystrophin to 5.46% at 6 months (muscle content-adjusted; p<0.0001), make an accelerated approval pathway much more likely.

Market Opportunity for Dyne and Valuation

First, let's talk about the market opportunity for DYNE-251. DMD is a relatively well-established commercial area. The small DMD population treatable with DYNE-251 is about 1600 boys. The drug will be priced at about 1M per year, as mentioned by the CEO at JPM 2026. Assuming a 20-30% penetration at peak adoption, given it is a very expensive specialty drug, annual peak sales would be $320-500M, which is significant.

For DYNE-101 treating DM1, it is harder to estimate since there are no fully FDA-approved, disease-modifying drugs specifically for it. However, based on the estimate that in the US there are about 140,000 DM1 patients, with 10% penetration and a $100,000/year price tag, peak sales can easily top $1B just in the US alone. In fact, analysts project that Dyne Therapeutics' DYNE-101, for DM1, could reach peak annual sales of approximately $4.3B by 2035, according to one source. However, it is important to know that DYNE-101 is on a traditional approval path, which will take longer to get approved compared to DYNE-251, which is on the trajectory for accelerated approval.

Given the strength of the data, I assign a conservative 50% approval rate for DYNE-251 and 60% for DYNE-101. Using a peak sale of 400M for Dyne-251 and 2B for DYNE-101, a risk-adjusted peak sale for the current 2 lead assets is 1.4B, making its current valuation of 2.75B look reasonable, even cheap considering the company has about 1B in cash.

2026 Catalysts timetable

a. DYNE-251: Accelerated Approval Submission

  • Q2 2026: Dyne plans to submit a Biologics License Application (BLA) for U.S. Accelerated Approval based on Phase 1/2 DELIVER trial data.
  • Q2 2026: Initiation of a global Phase 3 clinical trial for z-rostudirsen to support global and full approvals.

I do not expect huge price changes, as these are not binary catalysts, more just on execution. If they do not execute, expect further downside.

b. DYNE-101 (DM1): Trial execution

  • Q2 2026: Full enrollment in the ongoing Registrational Expansion Cohort of the ACHIEVE trial. Note that this was delayed by 2 quarters, so it makes one question Dyne's trial execution ability.
  • Q1 2026: Initiate a confirmatory Phase 3 clinical trial in Q1 2026

I also do not expect huge price changes, as these are not data readouts. But it is crucial they adhere to the timeline and avoid further delay, since their competitor Avidity is ahead of the game, as seen below.

c. External Market Catalyst

  • H2 2026: Anticipated Phase 3 trial readout for Avidity Biosciences' del-desiran, a direct competitor in the DM1 space, which analysts expect to significantly impact Dyne's stock.

Due to design and mechanism similarities between Avidity's del-desiran and Dyne Therapeutics' DYNE-101, a successful Phase 3 readout for del-desiran would significantly de-risk DYNE-101, potentially raising its approval probability to the 80-90% range. Following strong Phase 1/2 DM1 data from both companies, a positive Phase 3 outcome is anticipated. This readout serves as the primary catalyst for Dyne in 2026; a successful competitor result could drive DYNE stock 30-50% above its current valuation (recorded at $16.71/share with a $2.75B market cap as of January 19, 2025).

Financial Health and Key Risks

Following a recent $350M equity offering, Dyne is projected to hold approximately $1B-$1.1B in cash and cash equivalents at the start of 2026. Given a 2025 burn rate of roughly $110M per quarter, this capital provides a cash runway into early 2028. This two-year liquidity position reflects a healthy balance sheet, supporting operations through several key milestones.

The key risk for Dyne in 2026 is poor execution. If Dyne is not able to meet these clinical program timelines, its competitor Avidity will pull forward even more, with more time to take market share away from Dyne in the near future.

Final Verdict

Dyne Therapeutics presents as a strong "buy" due to two promising programs supported by robust clinical data and clear regulatory pathways. The efficacy of its conjugation platform is validated, further bolstered by significant big pharma interest in this modality, highlighted by the recent $12B acquisition of competitor Avidity by Novartis. A key catalyst is the anticipated Avidity Phase 3 readout for its DM1 program in the second half of 2026. A positive result-which is likely-would significantly de-risk Dyne's own DYNE-101 candidate, likely driving a substantial stock price increase. As such, I assign a one-year price target of $25.

data.title: Fulgent Genetics: The Price Has Caught Up To Value, But I Still Like The Stock
data.description:
It's been over a year since I covered Fulgent Genetics (FLGT), a biotech company focusing on cancer cures. On my first look, I saw a company trading significantly below the net value of its cash and marketable securities, which had a few drug candidates in the pipeline, giving downside protection with the possibility of upside. I also liked that the company was heavily owned by insiders and that by investing in Fulgent, investors could align their money with a competent management team led by Ming Hsieh, the longtime CEO who owns more than 28% of the company.

A year later, the story is largely the same, but the stock price has risen nearly 50% (versus 15% for the S&P 500), begging the question of whether FLGT is still undervalued.

I see someone buying a share of FLGT stock as buying three distinct things: 1) a pile of cash and liquid assets, mostly U.S. government debt like T-bills; 2) a fully functional lab diagnostics business, which is growing; and 3) two drug candidates, which (like any drug in development) are burning cash in R&D costs but could provide big returns down the road. This article will give updates on all three of those components of Fulgent's story, as well as reiterate why I'm a fan of the management team. Let's jump right in.

Financials

I think of FLGT as being largely an asset story, so let us start with the balance sheet. As reported in the Q3 2025 earnings, stockholders' equity was down slightly, less than 1%, from the beginning of the year, sitting at $1.12 billion. Fulgent has no debt or meaningful long-term liabilities for that matter. The assets were basically the same as at the start of the year, although with a larger share of cash and short-term securities relative to long-term securities. Cash sits at $117 million, short-term marketable securities were $258 million, and long-term securities made up $411 million.

In my last article, I kept things simple, calculating a conservative figure for Fulgent's net asset value by starting with book value and subtracting all goodwill and intangible assets. The $1.12 billion in book value, minus $25m in goodwill and $135m in intangible assets, leaves $960m in net assets. That comes out to $31.07 on a per-share basis, which is 10% above the stock price at the time of writing, $28.17.

To be even more stingy, we could take the $111 million in fixed assets and assume that in the case of liquidation, they would be worth 50% of their balance sheet account. The four largest categories in the fixed assets account are medical lab equipment, building improvements, buildings, and land. So taking away another $56m in assets leaves us with $29.26 in tangible assets, less than 4% above the current stock price. To be clear, that is a relatively conservative net asset value.

Let's move on to the lab business. All of Fulgent's revenue comes from laboratory services, which breaks into three segments: precision diagnostics, anatomic pathology, and BioPharma services. Over the first nine months of 2025, FLGT's top line revenue increased 15.4% from $207m to $239m. For context, peer company Quest Diagnostics (DGX) saw 13.4% revenue growth over the same period. Fulgent's growth came across all categories: precision diagnostics increased by 14.5%, anatomic pathology by 12.2%, and BioPharma services by 72%. Precision diagnostics makes up the majority of revenues, coming in at $142m, or 59% of revenues, for the nine months ended September 30, 2025. Anatomic pathology was $89m, or 33% of revenues, and BioPharma services was the smallest at $17.5m, or 7.3% of revenues.

The therapeutic development business (aka Fulgent Pharma) is all about curing cancers or improving the ability to treat them. There are three drug candidates.

FID-007, which aims to treat head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC), is furthest along, currently in Phase 2 of clinical trials, while FID-022 is for cancers including colon, bile duct, ovarian, and pancreatic cancers and is in Phase 1. ADCs, or antibody-drug conjugates, are in the preclinical stage.

Fulgent's next earnings release is estimated by Zacks to be February 27th, 2026. Management has guided for $325 million in total revenue in 2025 and a target cash burn of $25 million in the therapeutic development segment, which they expect could roughly double to between $35m and $50m in 2026, according to remarks made at the UBS Global Healthcare Conference 2025.

Management

It isn't much of an exaggeration to say Fulgent Genetics is Ming Hsieh's company. As of the proxy statement released in March 2025, Hsieh owns 28.43% of the common stock. Hanlin Gao, who serves as Chief Scientific Officer, accounts for 2.85%. The entire group of directors and executive officers combined for 33.11% of the outstanding stock (up from 32.66% the year prior).

FLGT has repurchased nearly $11 million worth of stock in 2025, according to the Q3 earnings. Most of these buybacks ($8.7 million) happened in the first quarter of 2025. From the footnotes: "During the three months ended March 31, 2025, the Company repurchased 0.5 million shares of its common stock at an aggregate cost of $7.9 million." That comes out to an average price paid of ~$15.8 per share. Coincidentally, this is almost the same price paid by CFO Kim Paul when he bought $1.6 million worth of stock. I think repurchasing shares at such a price was opportunistic by management and indicates to me that stock repurchases are done with value in mind, not just for the sake of doing them. As of September 30, 2025, $139.6 million remained available for future repurchases under the current stock repurchase program.

On December 22, 2025, Fulgent announced a $55.5 million acquisition of Bako Diagnostics and StrataDx. The deal is all cash and appears to be a pretty standard bolt-on for a diagnostics company. The deal is hard to evaluate with limited information, but viewed in the context that Fulgent's lab services business is already growing organically, I'm willing to give management the benefit of the doubt. Bako's facilities are in Georgia, and StrataDx's are in Massachusetts. From the footnotes to the latest 10-K, we know Fulgent already had a presence in both states:

The Company’s headquarters are located in El Monte, California, which is comprised of various corporate offices and a laboratory... Other CLIA-certified laboratories are located in Coppell, Texas; Needham, Massachusetts; Phoenix, Arizona; Alpharetta, Georgia.

Management foreshadowed this acquisition at the UBS Global Healthcare Conference in November of 2025. CFO Paul Kim said, "I think the possibility of M&A is definitely in the picture." Brandon Perthuis, Fulgent's Chief Commercial Officer, reiterated the sentiment:

We believe there's other targets out there where we can do similar things, where we can take the Fulgent technology platform and put those in businesses to improve those businesses. So M&A is a top priority for us. But if we do another one, we want to make sure it's the right one and a high probability of success. And it's probably just a matter of time.

Recall that Fulgent's cash pile was built up from its COVID testing segment; Fulgent created COVID tests shortly after the pandemic started and was heavily profitable. Adding up everything I see with Fulgent (the opportunistic COVID testing segment, holding the cash in low-risk securities, the buybacks, and the high percentage of insider ownership) gives me a sunny view on management. As the stock price catches up to the net asset value, I think Fulgent increasingly becomes a management story over an asset play.

Risks

The main risks I see with Fulgent are fairly straightforward. First, there is the risk that the drug candidates don't pan out. Management has guided toward higher development costs next year (roughly $35-50m projected in 2026 compared to $25m expected in 2025), and this could continue to burn cash as it offsets any growth in lab services. Additionally, as interest rates go lower, the benefit to holding cash decreases. Since I've argued that by investing in FLGT, investors are deciding to own a pile of cash and treasuries, the risk of inflation is present here. So increased cash burn from R&D combined with macro risks means that there is a good chance the cash pile we are looking at today will be worth less tomorrow in both real and nominal terms.

And to play devil's advocate to my points about management, I'll point out the $120m goodwill impairment expense in 2023, shedding doubt on management's M&A record. Additionally, I just praised the ~$10 million repurchase of shares in 2025 at a discount from today's price, but Fulgent also repurchased shares in previous years at a premium. According to those same notes to the 2024 annual report, in 2022, $74.3 million worth of shares were repurchased at ~$41 per share. In my estimation, there is nuance between opportunistically repurchasing shares and returning cash to shareholders, and so I don't always see repurchases as a mistake if they were done at a higher price. But by praising buybacks done at a discount to today's price, I might be allowing myself a double standard in how I grade Fulgent's management.

Conclusion

I don't have any advanced qualifications in biology, and therefore I don't expect to find much of an informational edge in the intricacies of the diagnostics business or the drug candidates. If you're looking for a deeper dive on the pharma side of things, Stock Research Platform wrote a deep dive on Fulgent in September 2025 that goes into more detail. In this article I've tried to provide sufficient coverage of the drug candidates and lab business and add insights on management and the valuation that I haven't seen covered yet.

The way I see it, by buying shares in Fulgent Genetics, investors are getting three things, in this order:

- A pile of cash and safe, low-yield bonds

- A growing diagnostics business

- Two drug candidates in development that will only pay out in the long run, if they pay out at all.

That's basically the same story I saw when I looked at Fulgent a year ago, and I expect it to be the same story a year from now. What's changed is the numbers, especially the stock price. The gap between the stock price and the intrinsic value has narrowed, and accordingly, Fulgent is a less attractive opportunity.

I debated between a Hold or Buy rating for Fulgent. I think it comes down to personal preference. For me, there is something satisfying about a stock with an intuitive story that I can understand. The feeling that I am buying a pile of cash and short-term Treasuries at fair value and getting the upside of the lab business and drug candidates for free scratches an itch in a part of my brain. On the other hand, I don't see much potential for upside in the short term.

I decided to keep a Buy rating on the stock, with the understanding that it is a weaker buy than it was a year ago because it is no longer as cheap. But the story is the same, and it is positive: the balance sheet is a fortress, and I like what I'm seeing from a management group that is heavily aligned with shareholders. The only consideration with Fulgent is the opportunity cost. I may trim my position if the price continues to creep up, and I'd be happy to scoop up more if it went on sale, but for now, I'll be holding my shares. With Fulgent Genetics, I came for the discount to net assets, and I'm staying for the management.

data.title: SHOC: A Buy For Risk-Tolerant Growth Investors
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Strive U.S. Semiconductor ETF (SHOC) warrants a buy rating due to its strong mix of top semiconductor companies that are demonstrating accelerating growth with competitive advantages in the industry. Furthermore, the fund has a competitive expense ratio and reasonable valuation. However, I prefer VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) due to its inclusion of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) and its slightly lower fees.

Strive U.S. Semiconductor ETF—Overview and Compared ETFs

SHOC is a passively managed ETF that seeks to capture a broad cap-weighted list of U.S. semiconductor companies. Therefore, SHOC excludes many international holdings such as TSM. With its inception in 2022, the fund has 31 holdings and $154M in AUM.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are VanEck Semiconductor ETF, State Street® SPDR® S&P® Semiconductor ETF (XSD), and Invesco Semiconductors ETF (PSI). SMH is a popular fund that seeks to capture the largest semiconductor companies in both the United States and around the world. I wrote on SMH in early 2024 and rated the fund a strong buy, which I still maintain. XSD differs from all other funds in that it uses a modified equal-weight index to capture a mixture of large, mid, and small-cap semiconductor companies. PSI captures the top 30 U.S. semiconductor companies based on criteria including price and earnings momentum, quality, and value.

Funds Compared: Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

SHOC has an impressive 3-year average annualized return of 41.41%. XSD saw the worst 3-year performance with an average annual return of 24.67%. However, this performance was still superior to the broader market, as measured by the S&P 500 Index over the past three years. PSI also saw a strong 3-year average return of 33.48%. SMH saw the best 3-year performance with an average return of 53.17%.

SHOC’s expense ratio of 0.40% is competitive among peer funds. However, SMH and XSD have slightly lower fees. Because most semiconductor companies focus on high levels of growth, few offer significant dividends. As a result, SHOC offers a meager 0.21% yield, which is comparable to competing funds.

SHOC Holdings and Key Outlook Factors

Over 90% of SHOC’s weight is in U.S. holdings. The remainder of the fund's weight is in European companies. This is a distinction from ETFs like SMH, which include holdings in Taiwan. SHOC’s lack of TSM is a noteworthy differentiator that I will cover later.

SHOC is a highly concentrated fund with 76.2% in its top 10 holdings. Additionally, SHOC holds over 40% of its weight in just its top three holdings. Looking forward, there are key strengths across several of the fund’s top-weighted companies, which I detail below.

Nvidia: Data Center Spending Will Accelerate Revenue through at least 2030

The first key outlook factor is NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) at nearly 20% weight. This weight is comparable to the VanEck fund, SMH, but represents a key distinction from XSD and PSI. A key competitive advantage for Nvidia looking forward is its repositioning from a chip company to a company that offers AI data infrastructure. As a result, Nvidia’s future revenue will be directly correlated with data center capital expenditure. Nvidia expects a 40% CAGR in data center capital expenditure, fueled by Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), OpenAI, and many other companies. This expectation is supported by industry trends. Specifically, the power demand for data centers is expected to reach 4% of global power demand by 2030.

To add to its growth prospects, Nvidia’s current valuation is high but not unattractive. The chip company’s forward P/E is 28% below its 5-year average and only 30% higher than its sector median. Given its unique value proposition and accelerated growth, I see Nvidia as an attractive buy and a key asset as part of SHOC’s top holdings. Given that the company has seen 65% YoY revenue growth and is highly profitable with a 53% net income margin, NVDA will likely remain SHOC’s top holding for the foreseeable future.

Broadcom: Diverse Revenue Streams will Continue to Fuel Growth

The second key factor to consider is Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) at about 14.5% weight. This represents the highest weight of all the funds compared. AVGO is another strong holding for SHOC due to its diverse revenue streams that continue to drive strong fundamentals. While over half of Broadcom’s revenue comes from semiconductor solutions, including AI XPUs and AI networking, another significant portion comes from infrastructure software, including cybersecurity, private cloud systems, and fiber channel networking. This set of diverse streams has led to the highest net revenue and adjusted EBITDA for the company in FY25.

Broadcom’s outlook also looks bright. With a record high free cash flow for the company of almost $27B, AVGO has room to fuel its $11B in research and development. Alternatively, Broadcom may continue its dividend growth, which has seen a CAGR of 30% over the past decade. AVGO’s valuation, like NVDA's, is also high but not unwarranted, with a forward P/E of 31. Therefore, Broadcom represents another strong holding for SHOC looking forward.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company: A Critical Gap for SHOC

SHOC’s #3 holding, Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), takes up over 6% weight. However, all compared funds have between 3.9% to 6.9% weight in Micron. Therefore, MU is not a significant differentiator for SHOC looking forward. Instead, the third key variable for SHOC is its lack of TSM. TSM’s recent fundamentals have been impressive, with a 31% YoY revenue growth and 45% net income margin. Looking forward, TSM’s 3nm chip technology is a critical competitive advantage. These chips represent a large leap forward in both faster speeds and reduced power requirements. TSM has already seen an explosion in 3nm demand in the last few quarters.

In addition to 3nm technology, TSM is opening additional fabrication facilities around the world to meet increasing demand. Despite a one-year share price return of 49%, TSM is still attractively valued. With a forward P/E of around 23, the company is one of the most favorably valued holdings for SMH. Therefore, the lack of TSM in SHOC’s top-weighted holdings represents a key disadvantage for the ETF.

Current Valuation and 2026 Outlook

All compared semiconductor funds have outperformed the S&P 500 Index over the past year. SHOC has even outperformed peer semiconductor ETFs due to strong recent performances by Micron Technologies and Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) with 281% and 168% one-year returns, respectively.

Due to its strong one-year return, SHOC has the least desirable valuation compared to semiconductor funds when examining its P/E and P/B ratios. However, given the unique mix of top holdings that will likely persist for the coming years, SHOC still warrants a buy rating. Furthermore, due to the growth and profitability characteristics of the holdings discussed for SHOC, I expect the fund to continue to see market-beating returns in 2026.

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 25 January, 2026.

Risks to Investors

In addition to the concentration risk previously discussed, supply chain risk remains a key factor for investors in the semiconductor industry. For example, Nvidia and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company are vitally linked. Last year, Nvidia’s demand needs comprised 70% of TSMC’s advanced chip manufacturing capacity. This concentration is due to TSMC’s leadership in extreme ultraviolet lithography, which gives it a unique supplier position for global AI-related demand.

Given this supply dynamic, TSMC is one example of supply chain risk for many of SHOC’s top holdings. Therefore, while SHOC predominantly includes U.S. holdings, its reliance on companies like TSMC both increases risk and also reduces any potential advantage over global semiconductor funds like SMH.

Concluding Summary

Leading companies within the semiconductor industry will likely see strong growth, looking forward to fulfilling AI and data center demand. SHOC is worthy of a buy rating as the fund will capture this growth and thereby provide investors with significant capital appreciation. However, I still prefer SMH due to its inclusion of TSM and slightly lower fees. Despite unique risk factors such as industry-specific concentration and supply chain risk, funds like SHOC warrant a place in an investor’s portfolio if they are willing to take on additional risk for strong growth potential.

data.title: Turning VanEck's 2026 Outlook Into Portfolio Allocations
data.description:
CEO Jan van Eck’s 2026 outlook highlights where visibility is improving. Here we show how we express those views in portfolios.

“Visibility means risk on” is the headline of CEO Jan van Eck’s 2026 investment outlook. While our views are grounded in long-term structural changes rather than short-term market calls, a defining feature of VanEck’s model portfolio approach is the ability to remain adaptive, seeking to participate in upside while actively managing risk across both frothy markets and oversold conditions.

In this blog, we translate Jan’s 2026 outlook into portfolio implementation. We’ll outline the macro backdrop and show how we are allocating across the key theme within our model portfolios.

Three Themes Driving 2026 Positioning

VanEck’s investment framework is built around identifying durable themes that shape markets over time. Heading into 2026, three themes sit at the core of our portfolio construction:

  • Debasement: The global search for alternative stores of value and diversification away from the US dollar amid elevated debt, deficits, conflict and inflationary pressures.
  • Digitalization: The continued expansion of AI as it moves from infrastructure build-out to adoption and ultimately proliferation, reshaping productivity across industries.
  • Decarbonization: Less about energy transition and alternative energy, and more about energy addition, focusing on capacity, reliability and efficiency as the world demands more power to support electrification, AI and growth.

These themes form the foundation for how we think about asset allocation in 2026.

Macro Dynamics Reinforcing Our 2026 Themes

The Federal Reserve (Fed) remains caught between the risk of reaccelerating inflation and a potential inflection in the labor market. While labor conditions have softened, they have not deteriorated enough to warrant significant accommodation. Inflation remains above the Fed’s 2% target, and a neutral rate in the 3% range seems prudent in an environment of slightly elevated inflation, assuming nominal growth continues to be supported by policy and fiscal support.

At the same time, debt and deficit dynamics are contributing to a weaker US dollar and a flatter yield curve with elevated long-term rates. While near-term fiscal restraint may improve headline deficit numbers, the structural problem is not going away. In a highly politicized environment, we expect policy headlines to create volatility across both equity and fixed income markets, particularly in a historically volatile midterm election year.

Another notable dynamic is the K-shaped economy. Spending, wage growth and asset ownership remain concentrated in the higher income cohorts, while inflation, housing affordability and student debt are continuing to pressure younger people and lower-wage earners. Policies aimed at addressing the bottom of the K is likely to be deficit expansionary and potentially inflationary, but also may play a decisive role in upcoming elections.

As we discussed in Jan’s outlook last year, the world has been over-indexed to the US dollar for over a decade. That dynamic began to shift in 2025, as some of the best-performing assets included precious metals and international equities. Diversification remains a powerful tool for managing risk, particularly as returns become more concentrated, and investors may benefit from reassessing where risk and returns are truly coming from in their portfolios. If a small handful of companies are driving the majority of returns in a “globally diversified” portfolio, it may be time for a portfolio review.

Translating Themes to Allocations in Our Wealth Builder Framework

VanEck’s Wealth Builder Core Portfolios and Plus Portfolios are designed to translate these macro and thematic views into diversified, multi-asset allocations. While the models maintain long-term structural tilts, they are also built to be adaptive by rebalancing, managing exposure after rallies and adding opportunistically during periods of dislocation.

Risk management is central to this process. Using ETFs within the model portfolios as implementation vehicles, the models seek diversified exposure across asset classes, regions, and risk factors.

Debasement and the Case for Gold

There is an old adage in investing: “Don’t fight the Fed.” But what happens when there are 20 of them? There are over 20 global central banks today actively diversifying their reserves, with gold playing a central role in their efforts. In addition to central bank demand, gold has benefited from its safe-haven characteristics and diversification benefits, as traditional stock and bond indexes trade near historical highs.

Within the Wealth Builder model portfolios, we maintain exposure to both gold bullion through the VanEck Merk Gold ETF (OUNZ) and gold equities through the VanEck Real Assets ETF (RAAX). Physical gold bullion, with its lower volatility profile and diversification characteristic, represents a larger share of exposure relative to gold mining equities, which historically exhibit higher volatility and a higher correlation to equities.

With risk management as key to our process, as gold prices moved higher, the models capitalized on upside volatility to trim exposure into strength, while remaining structurally overweight relative to traditional benchmarks.

Digitization Beyond the AI Hype Cycle

AI adoption continues to accelerate at a historically unprecedented pace. With such rapid growth, market forces will naturally question the sustainability of the valuations. Instead of reacting to the boom-bust hype, we continue to view AI adoption through our three phase model.

Phase one is the infrastructure arms race, where hyperscalers invest aggressively to avoid falling behind competitors. At the end of 2025, expectations began to reset as markets punished companies facing rapid debt accumulation, slowing earnings and weaker guidance. As irrational exuberance gives way to more rational growth expectations, a company’s ability to show both growth and profitability by generating returns above the cost of capital becomes increasingly important.

Phase two is the adoption phase, where AI agents move meaningfully into everyday workflows, driving efficiency gains and productivity improvements that support profitability and margins. A few leaders are moving into this phase.

Phase 3 is the automation phase, where the convergence of AI and robotics redefine labor and productivity, and the first signs of this are beginning to appear. Each of these phases has specific company, sector and geographic dynamics to consider, reinforcing the importance of diversified exposure.

Within the Wealth Builder models, AI exposure is expressed through a combination of semiconductor companies, which we hold through the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH), broader technology allocations and emerging areas like quantum computing. At the same time, we find it prudent to own complementary exposures that are less sensitive to AI-driven volatility, including defense, infrastructure and critical metals. These long-cycle growth themes are supported by policy, geopolitics and supply chain fundamentals, and historically exhibit lower correlations to pure-play AI returns.

Decarbonization as Energy Addition

Even without AI, global energy and infrastructure were already facing a structural supply-demand imbalance. AI has acted as an accelerant, significantly increasing projected power demand as data centers and advanced computing expand. Old world assets, like raw materials and commodities, needed to build new world infrastructure and the digital economy.

Nuclear power is central to this theme due to its high capacity factor, competitive cost, cleaner carbon profile and more reliable uptime relative to other alternative energy sources. As the market grapple with valuation concerns amid the massive investment implications, our Wealth Builder models have actively managed exposure, trimming during rallies and adding during periods of dislocation, to maintain long-term conviction in this theme. For exposure across the nuclear value chain, the model holds the VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF (NLR).

Income Positioning in a Changing Credit Landscape

Outside of equities we continue to see selective opportunities in income-oriented assets, particularly as private credit competes for investor attention and capital. Crowding into less liquid segments of the credit market has pushed some investors toward riskier corners of credit. Early cracks in this boom cycle were felt in 2025 with the bankruptcies of First Brands and TriColor, which sparked calls for a broader acceleration of defaults and downgrades.

Publicly traded business development companies (BDCs), given their liquidity and transparency, acted as a release valve for investors looking to reduce private credit exposure. The resulting selloff pushed valuations below book value across much of the sector, creating a potentially attractive setup for income investors for 2026. VanEck’s Wealth Builder portfolios hold the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), which the models use to gain exposure to a diversified index of BDCs and can be used as a complement or replacement for private credit and leveraged loans.

Turning Visibility into Action

“Visibility means risk on” underscores the importance of disciplined diversification, active risk management and thoughtful implementation. By anchoring portfolios around the themes of debasement, digitization and decarbonization, we believe investors can participate in long-term opportunities while remaining mindful of evolving risks. VanEck’s Wealth Builder model portfolios offer a way to put that framework into practice in 2026.

PMI – Purchasing Managers’ Index: economic indicators derived from monthly surveys of private sector companies. A reading above 50 indicates expansion, and a reading below 50 indicates contraction; ISM – Institute for Supply Management PMI: ISM releases an index based on more than 400 purchasing and supply managers surveys; both in the manufacturing and non-manufacturing industries; CPI – Consumer Price Index: an index of the variation in prices paid by typical consumers for retail goods and other items; PPI – Producer Price Index: a family of indexes that measures the average change in selling prices received by domestic producers of goods and services over time; PCE inflation – Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index: one measure of U.S. inflation, tracking the change in prices of goods and services purchased by consumers throughout the economy; MSCI – Morgan Stanley Capital International: an American provider of equity, fixed income, hedge fund stock market indexes, and equity portfolio analysis tools; VIX – CBOE Volatility Index: an index created by the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE), which shows the market's expectation of 30-day volatility. data.title: Highwood Value Partners H2 2025 Portfolio Changes
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Highwood Value Partners H2 2025 Letter To Investors.

Changes in the Portfolio – Realized Gains and Concentration

In the second half of 2025, we sold our positions in Protector (PSKRF), Ryanair (RYAAY) and Alimak. As such, we have now exited every position I started the firm with in December 2019. With these sales, our stated returns (+76% since inception) are almost entirely realized returns as noted in the table on page one. I will take a moment here to provide the score card on this cohort of investments.

I started the firm with investments in the shares of five companies – Protector, Ryanair, Alimak, Standard Drilling and Vestas Wind Systems (VWDRY). We held these investments for 4.7 years on average and realized 2.0x money and a 16% IRR on average across the group. More pleasingly is that we did not lose money on a single investment. Our lowest return investment was Standard Drilling ASA (1.2x money/8% 2yr IRR) and our best result was in Protector Forsikring (3.6x money/50% 6yr IRR).

While our returns on Protector were top of the class, we can learn from our experience with this investment as well. We achieved 3.6x money on our investment while the shares were up 7.9x including dividends over the same period. This is the result of my decision to trim this holding at various points over the past 5 years to fit the constraints on position sizing I set at inception. Hindsight is 20/20 and we must be careful not to draw the wrong conclusions from this experience, however we have taken learnings from this one and now carry that forward.

I commented on the rationale for the sale of Standard Drilling here and Vestas here. A brief word on the sales of our shares in Ryanair and Alimak now follows.

  • Ryanair. Our original thesis on Ryanair in 2019, available here, could be boiled down to the conviction that the business was highly likely to deliver c.€2 of earnings per share in the medium term, and exiting at 15x earnings would result in a double in the share price. On my initial analysis, which did not envision a war in Europe or a pandemic, this was most likely in 2023/2024. In the end, it took a bit longer, but Ryanair delivered €2 in earnings per share and the shares re-rated to c.15x earnings, resulting in a share price of c.€30. We took that opportunity to crystallize our gains. The thesis played out and we exited on the basis of valuation. The company continues to demonstrate the qualities we value as long term investors, but the price for that economic productivity is no longer as attractive.
  • Alimak. Our shares in Alimak have been a strong performer over the past two years, up 80% in local currency. We exited at 19x earnings, which was in the realm of fair value. Similar to Ryanair, the sale decision was the result of our valuation discipline rather than anything changing in the fundamentals that we did not like.

Our portfolio is now more concentrated than at any point in its history. I have re-deployed a portion of the proceeds from the sales above into existing positions where (a) the thesis is playing out as expected, (b) the discount to fair value has widened and (c) catalysts for re-valuation are more visible. As of the date of this letter, our three largest positions make up 37% of our capital and our five largest make up 57% of the portfolio. Meanwhile, we have significant dry powder to act on new ideas in the pipeline.

data.title: Greenlight Capital Re: Valuation Stuck Below Book Value Until The Uncertainties Clear
data.description:
Greenlight Capital Re, Ltd. (GLRE) is an investment vehicle designed to serve David Einhorn's investment purposes. The reinsurer could be considered a small (and less successful) replica of Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A)(BRK.B).

Still, history has proven that becoming the new Warren Buffett is not an easy task. And David Einhorn, despite all his prior investment successes, has struggled to outperform the regular indexes.

Nasdaq and S&P 500 indexes have generated a 21% and 19% return for 2025.

GLRE has been less satisfying for the investors, with a meager 4% return.

Despite improvements highlighted in my latest coverage, the reinsurer is penalized by two effects:

  • The company is a third-tier reinsurance carrier with poor underwriting discipline and is suffering from the volatility of reinsurance cycles.
  • A large portion of the available assets is invested in Solasglas, a dedicated investment fund.

Both uncertainties are blocking—and locking—the company’s valuation.

A Brief Intro To Refresh Your Mind

Greenlight Re may be a relatively small reinsurer, but it operates across three key jurisdictions. Its structure looks like this:

Why these three locations?

  • Cayman Islands – It all started here in 2004. The Cayman Islands offer a straightforward regulatory framework and… let’s be honest, attractive tax treatment.
  • Ireland – Also tax-friendly, but more importantly, Ireland has become a hub for reinsurers thanks to its EU access and deep talent pool. If you want to tap into the European insurance ecosystem, Dublin is hard to ignore.
  • United Kingdom – London is the spiritual home of insurance and reinsurance. Lloyd’s is still the global marketplace for niche and specialty risk. If you want access to syndicates, brokers, and international business, you need a footprint in King Charles III’s backyard.

In short: Cayman for setup and efficiency, Ireland for EU access and talent, and the UK for Lloyd’s and international reach. It’s a classic trifecta in reinsurance land.

What Does Greenlight Do?

The reinsurance division is divided into two branches: the open market segment and the innovations segment.

The open market segment is a traditional reinsurance division operating in several fronts: property, casualty, health, and specialty.

The innovations segment provides reinsurance capacities to start-up companies and managing general agents (called MGAs).

In addition to providing reinsurance capacity on a case-by-case basis, this segment invests in unlisted equities (mostly preferred shares) and convertible debt instruments. The investment side had been so successful that the company was reversing a $16 million unrealized gain in 09M2025—a neat reminder that private valuations can rise fast but fall just as hard.

Both segments were profitable over the first nine months of the year. But that wasn’t always the case. Greenlight Re has a solid track record of underperformance on the underwriting side, with combined ratios consistently above 100%.

Over the last five years, Greenlight’s reinsurance business has only been technically profitable once—in 2023.

Does that mean 2025 is destined to look like 2020, 2022, and 2024? Not necessarily.

The underwriting margin this year is likely to be extremely thin—somewhere between 97% and 99%, and realistically closer to 99% than 97%. Still, the reasonably good performance in the first nine months means the reinsurance portfolio could finish the year in positive territory.

In short, the track record isn’t pretty, but 2025 isn’t condemned to repeat history—at least not yet.

I do want to believe that GLRE’s underwriting discipline will improve in the coming years. But the prior track record does not exactly play in GLRE’s favor. With the improved underwriting margins, a transformation may be underway. It’s a wait-and-see situation—and frankly, the bigger issues lie elsewhere.

A Poor-Performing Investment Portfolio

Greenlight Re’s implicit promise is simple: we collect reinsurance premiums, and then we put David Einhorn’s (widely recognized) investment talent to work to generate outperformance.

Unfortunately, when I look at the recent results, the whole setup feels less like a well-oiled investment machine… and more like Fantasia—with the Sorcerer’s Apprentice frantically trying to control the magic he set loose.

In the latest investor presentation, Solasglas is presented as the key driver of Greenlight's book value growth. The return is pretty decent, but a plain-vanilla index would have done better.

I keep coming back to the same thought: through Greenlight Re, investors are essentially financing David Einhorn’s investment decisions—wins and losses included. As of December 2025, the fund was up 4.4% year-to-date.

The fund’s performance was "decent", but the S&P 500 and Nasdaq returned 15% to 20% in 2025. Once again, investing in GLRE to benefit from David Einhorn’s fund management does not appear to be a valid option.

A Lose-Lose Situation

Greenlight Re is facing two problems.

First, underwriting performance remains volatile and generally weak, mostly negative, even if 2025 might end up close to breakeven.

Second, the implicit promise of long-term outperformance through the Solasglas/DME investment portfolios has not been delivered.

In other words, we have a reinsurer that struggles to make money on underwriting and an investment engine that doesn’t consistently beat the indexes.

So the question becomes: why invest in Greenlight Re at all?

Investing in GLRE: Okay, But Know The Risks

Investing in GLRE is betting that the book value discount will reduce over the coming months or years. As of now, the company is traded at around 0.69x the book value.

A decent insurer/reinsurer can expect to be valued at 1 to 1.5x the book value. According to the SA scorecard, the industry’s average P/B ratio is approximately 1.3.

Since 2020, GLRE's valuation has never exceeded 0.8x the book value, as the investors lack confidence in GLRE's outlook.

The main drivers of the poor confidence of the investors are related to the two points mentioned at the beginning of my article: the poor underwriting performance, combined with the volatile results from the investment portfolio. I could also add a lack of catalysts for investors who are used to spotting potential upside. In GLRE’s case, there is no clear catalyst, even though the company’s recent underwriting results were better than in prior years.

Light At the End Of The Tunnel?

Aware of its "bad reputation", GLRE has decided to change its capital allocation via share repurchases. I won’t say that GLRE is a cannibal devouring its own shares, but the company has been quite active in 2025.

In Q3, the reinsurer repurchased $2.0 million of shares at an average cost of $12.88 per share, while the fully diluted book value per share was $18.90. For the first six months of the year, the company repurchased 357,278 ordinary shares for $5.0 million.

In total, the company has spent $7 million reducing the number of outstanding shares. I believe that an additional $2 million will be spent during the fourth quarter to continue this capital redistribution process, as the company could repurchase up to $25 million of shares by the end of June 2026.

To me, it’s a favorable sign to shareholders: “We know we messed up in the past, but now we intend to do better for you.”

Risks & Catalysts

Risks

Underwriting volatility: GLRE is not known as a pricing leader and has historically lacked a consistently strong underwriting discipline. Combined with potential increased severity and frequency of catastrophe losses, the combined ratio could come under pressure, resulting in underwriting losses. According to the latest outlook from Fitch Ratings, the reinsurance industry’s combined ratio is expected to normalize.

As a small and less diversified player, GLRE could face higher underwriting margin deterioration than larger peers in FY2026.

Solasglas Performance: GLRE’s investment income is tied to Solasglas’ positions and David Einhorn’s investment decisions. Unfavorable outcomes – whether from long or short positions – could weigh on investment results.

Catalysts

Improved underwriting performance would directly support earnings. More favorable market conditions could also fuel investment income, and ultimately, post-tax results. In such a scenario, a re-rating could occur as the current discount to book value may no longer be justified.

Additional potential catalysts include additional share repurchases and consolidation within the reinsurance industry. However, the latter appears less likely, as GLRE is primarily used as an investment vehicle to collect premiums and deploy float into financial markets.

A Rating Upgrade: Be Patient, My Dears

I won’t upgrade GLRE as of now. Although the company has made efforts to restore underwriting margins, I don’t have enough data to assess whether this is a real turnaround or just a "luck" effect.

Share repurchase programs with a P/B below 1 are an efficient strategy for redistributing value to shareholders.

There is no green light from my side to actively purchase shares. Other investors could disagree, looking at GLRE’s 2024 stock performance, and say, "You miss the full story, Crick; GLRE is well-positioned to outperform the indexes."

I may miss the full story—the hidden plan or the unseen strategy. Still, if I had to keep one indicator for a P&C insurer/reinsurer, it would be the underwriting margin level and its consistency. Up to 2024, GLRE’s reinsurance portfolio was consistently loss-making. And I don’t invest in unprofitable (re)insurance carriers, even though the investment income offsets the underwriting losses. GLRE’s solvency is not endangered. GLRE’s valuation only reflects the uncertainties around the underwriting margin level and the dependency on Einhorn’s investment decisions.

Remove one of those uncertainties, and the stock could rise to the ~$18 level, or 1.0× book value. Remove both uncertainties, and the stock could skyrocket to a 1.5× P/B valuation, or ~$28, representing almost a 100%+ return.

To me, the current valuation is well-deserved. Removing one of the uncertainties, the stock would need an immediate re-rating.

data.title: Ouster: Solid Fundamentals, But Valuation Remains A Concern
data.description:
Introduction:

Ouster, Inc. (OUST) is a company that specializes in creating LiDAR sensors and related software tools that enable customers to get the most out of these hardware solutions.

The firm's digital LiDAR technology has helped the company shift into a new era, and upcoming developments of new SoC devices, along with a handful of exciting new partnerships, demonstrate that this business has solid potential over the long run.

However, I believe that shares trade at too rich a valuation at the moment, especially relative to some of its peers. Therefore, I am rating shares of Ouster a sell, with the potential for an upgrade in the future once the valuation becomes more reasonable, given the solid fundamentals of the business.

Business Description & Key Considerations:

Ouster's two primary products are the Ouster Sensor (OS), which is a scanning sensor, and the Digital Flash, which is a solid-state flash sensor. The former is used by companies like robotaxi operators who need equipment with a full 360-degree view to detect objects in every direction, whereas the latter is often purchased by automakers for use in advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS).

Both of these sensors are built on the firm's digital LiDAR technology, which is an architecture designed around using just two chips instead of the thousands of individual lasers found in analog LiDAR devices.

The first component of Ouster's digital LiDAR system is a system-on-a-chip (SoC) device with single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) detectors. Essentially, these chips can detect the smallest possible unit of light, even at far distances of over 200 meters.

The other components are Ouster's proprietary vertical cavity surface emitting lasers (VCSELs). This is massively beneficial compared to using legacy edge-emitting lasers, as VCSELs are printed onto a single chip (meaning they can be packed with far more lasers across a smaller surface area).

Additionally, since VCSELs are made through the typical semiconductor manufacturing process, it is not much more expensive to change the print pattern to include more individual lasers (meaning the cost of adding extra lines does not scale linearly).

The firm's current generation REV7 LiDAR sensors mark a significant improvement over the previous iteration of the company's sensors, as they are built around this dual-chip architecture, and they are capable of detecting objects that are nearly twice as far.

A major point of emphasis for Ouster as of late has been to scale the "software attached business," which refers to selling a full-stack solution to customers (both the LiDAR sensors and the AI software layers). There are a wide variety of companies that require both these sensors as well as a turnkey software solution, and Ouster is capable of fulfilling this need.

Gemini is Ouster's proprietary AI software layer that can take the data from the LiDAR sensors and conclude what kind of object it is detecting. Gemini is particularly useful for applications such as logistics yards, where a customer can deploy numerous REV7 sensors and use the software layer to track operations in real time.

BlueCity is the company's AI-enabled traffic management system, which incorporates the company's LiDAR sensors and advanced algorithms to improve traffic flows and give city planners insights as to how to improve intersections. There are a little over 300K intersections that have traffic lights in the U.S. alone; only a small portion of these intersections currently have LiDAR sensors installed in the vicinity.

Ouster has recently signed 7 deals to bring its sensors and BlueCity technology layer to new states, with the firm's distribution partners now covering the majority of U.S. intersections.

Working with other firms to deploy these solutions is incredibly beneficial for Ouster, as the company doesn't have to worry about selling its solutions to local municipalities individually, but instead merely has to win business with an established player who has likely already been involved in working on these traffic systems for decades.

Now, let's turn to some of the most exciting developments for Ouster moving forward. The company currently holds a key competitive advantage when it comes to supplying LiDAR sensors for drone companies that wish to sell their product to the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD).

Ouster has received the Blue UAS certification, which means that the DoD has vetted the company's hardware to ensure that it is safe, reliable, and, perhaps most importantly, American-made. This creates a sizable moat for the firm in this specific niche, as drone companies are highly incentivized to purchase LiDAR sensors from Ouster for use inside their equipment.

Furthermore, the military drone market is projected to grow at over 13% annually from now through 2032 to reach approximately $47.2 billion, with a portion of this spending being allocated to the sensors that allow the drones to work effectively (which Ouster can capitalize on).

Another important catalyst moving forward is Ouster's two new custom SoC devices, the L4 to power the OS series and the Chronos, which is designed for the company's Digital Flash line of products.

These innovations are projected to lead to a massive increase in the firm's TAM, and I believe that if Ouster can deliver and improve the performance of both categories of its LiDAR devices, they will experience substantial sales growth over the coming years.

My line of thinking here is simple, as improvements in the L4 chip will enable improvements for some of the more complex use cases (in areas such as autonomous driving and defense). Then, if the Chronos improves to the point where it is usable by automakers for applications such as L3 ADAS, Ouster will be able to take a slice of the pie in the automotive LiDAR market (which is expected to grow at a 34% CAGR from now through 2032).

Finally, I believe that one of the most promising applications of LiDAR technology is for use in autonomous vehicles (AVs). Ouster has so far established partnerships with a few promising startups that are attempting to capture market share in this space, including both Motional and May Mobility.

The company's current agreement with Motional is to act as the exclusive long-range LiDAR supplier for the AV firms' IONIQ 5 robotaxis through the end of the current year. It must be noted that this deal currently revolves around Ouster supplying Motional with its Alpha Prime VLS-128 sensors, which are set to be discontinued in 2026. This indicates that if the two companies are going to renew their current deal, Ouster would likely be supplying Motional with one of its REV7 sensors.

Additionally, Chinese LiDAR supplier Hesai Group (HSAI) is currently the exclusive supplier of short-range LiDAR sensors for Motional's IONIQ 5 vehicles. I think that there is potential that the agreement between Hesai and Motional unravels over the coming years, as the introduction of the SAFE LiDAR act would mean that U.S.-based robotaxi operators would not be able to integrate Chinese-made sensors into their vehicles, which opens the door for Ouster to become the primary supplier for Motional of not only long-range but also short-range sensors for its ever-expanding fleet of AVs.

The other major robotaxi player that Ouster has partnered with is May Mobility. Back in September of 2025, May Mobility officially launched its Toyota Sienna Autono-MaaS vehicles on the Lyft platform, and Ouster is one of the primary suppliers of LiDAR sensors that are being retrofitted onto these AVs.

Risks:

I believe that while there is certainly a lot to like here, there are a few notable risk factors worth monitoring. First, Ouster currently has over 1,000 unique end customers to whom it sells its LiDAR solutions; only around 10% of these customers have reached full-scale production (where they are purchasing thousands of sensors in bulk instead of merely testing the technology).

According to the most recent quarterly filing, it can take between six months and several years to reach full commercial production, with automakers in particular requiring long testing phases before they roll out vehicles equipped with sensors to end consumers.

In my view, this presents a dual-sided risk as the company is highly dependent upon a small subset of customers for the majority of its current revenue, and there is some degree of uncertainty as to whether or not the other ~90% of customers will ever exit the pilot phase and begin purchasing Ouster's solutions in bulk.

Furthermore, as noted in the latest 10-Q, many of Ouster's customers are "innovators and early technology adopters," which presents even more risk given that many of these startups will likely not succeed over the long term.

Another potential issue I see on the horizon is management's attitude surrounding humanoid robots and the development of LiDAR technology to assist firms in deploying this technology commercially.

To provide evidence to this point, Ouster's CEO Charles Pacala recently stated that "I think the humanoid thing is interesting, but I think it's going to take a couple of years to play out."

While it may take longer than expected for humanoid robots to become integral parts of the global economy, there are several examples that show we could be closer than expected to high adoption of this technology.

First, Tesla just announced plans to begin selling its humanoid robots by the end of next year, which will likely prompt others in the space to ramp up production and enter commercial markets. Additionally, Hyundai's humanoid robot, dubbed the Atlas, has been unveiled as the newest addition to the OEM's assembly lines.

Other prominent LiDAR suppliers, such as Hesai and Aeva Technologies, Inc. (AEVA), are diligently working on developing solutions that will work well for this form factor, and I believe this will pay dividends in the future once this technology becomes more advanced.

To me, it appears that Ouster is much more focused on present-day opportunities in traffic management and industrial operations, which may lead to improved sales and profitability in the near term but stall long-term growth if peers get more design wins with startup robotics companies.

Finally, I believe that the company's strategy to create a walled garden and not allow enterprise customers to mix and match Ouster's technology solutions (Gemini and BlueCity) with sensors from other LiDAR providers is a bit of a misstep.

While there is certainly some merit to creating a closed ecosystem, as it can help reduce churn and lock in customers, it also means that Ouster is effectively cutting out potential customers who may've purchased sensors from a competitor that are entirely incompatible with the company's software platforms.

Financials & Valuation:

Ouster reported strong third quarter results, with revenue growing 41% YoY and 13% QoQ to $39.5 million, driven by an increase in the number of sensors sold (although this was slightly offset by lower average selling prices).

Additionally, the company's cost of revenue only increased by around 32% YoY, helping gross margins expand ~400bps to 42% in Q3 FY25. The driving force behind this increase was lower expenses related to obsolete inventory. I believe that this trend should continue going forward, especially given that the company plans to stop selling the LiDAR devices from Velodyne this year.

By shifting its manufacturing focus to the company's digital sensor offerings, fewer sensors will likely become obsolete, and this should allow gross margins to continue rising.

Also, as the company continues to grow its software-as-a-service business, margins should steadily grow, given that selling subscription software licenses is a much more profitable endeavor than selling hardware.

Ouster's third quarter operating expenses increased by just over 7% YoY to $41 million, which was largely due to an 18% rise in R&D expenses (growing to $17.8 million). I view the relatively sizable growth in R&D costs to be justifiable, given that the firm is currently ramping up the development of its next-generation SoC devices.

Ouster's adjusted EBITDA loss for the quarter was approximately $10 million, which was roughly the same as Q3 FY24 but steeper than the most recent quarter (Q2 FY25). The main driver behind the sequential decline in adjusted EBITDA was the absence of $5.5 million worth of employee retention credits, which had previously lowered both COGS and operating expenses.

Finally, the company is currently guiding for Q4 FY25 sales to hit $41 million at the midpoint of the range, which would represent around 36% YoY growth if it materializes.

Now let's turn to the valuation of the stock. Ouster currently trades at around 6.2x EV to forward sales, which is well below one of its peers, Aeva Technologies, Inc. (AEVA). It is also higher than both of the other comparable companies I have selected, Hesai Group and Innoviz Technologies Ltd. (INVZ).

Furthermore, when glancing out a few years from now, it is projected that the company will not begin to generate GAAP profits until after FY27. While this is also true for both Aeva and Innoviz, other LiDAR makers like Hesai Group are already profitable on a GAAP basis, and that stock is trading at lower valuation multiples based on projected revenues.

Also, in the case of Innoviz, the company is expected to grow sales significantly faster over the next couple of years compared to Ouster, which helps to explain why it is trading at higher valuation multiples.

At the moment, I believe there is very little potential upside in shares of Ouster given that it will likely take years for both sales and earnings to catch up to the present valuation (unless the company grows significantly faster than I currently anticipate it to).

This is not to mention the fact that the company is facing some headwinds related to both the uncertainty of future customer conversions as well as increasing competition in the humanoid robot space, as I expounded upon earlier.

Conclusion:

Ouster has been a solid performer as of late, with third quarter results beating expectations and guidance remaining impressive heading into the fourth quarter print in a couple of weeks.

There also appears to be a lot to look forward to on the horizon, including a few partnerships with robotaxi operators and the U.S. DoD to boot. However, I cannot look past the sky-high valuation, which to me indicates that investor enthusiasm is already exceeding my expectations for future performance of the company.

Given all of this, along with the fact that I view other companies in the space to be slightly better deals at the moment (see my article on Hesai Group here), I am rating shares of Ouster a Sell.

data.title: Firefly Aerospace: A $1.3B Backlog, Interesting, But Not Enough
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Firefly Aerospace (FLY) appears to have regained traction after its failed rocket mission back in September.

However, the trouble I have is that once we look beyond its alluring narrative, its fundamentals rapidly break down.

For one, its growth rates are erratic and challenging to forecast. Secondly, its management team doesn't have a lot of skin in the game. And lastly, but perhaps most importantly, the business is far from breakeven on its free cash flow line.

That being said, it's not all bad. As you know, the stock has already lost a lot of steam post-IPO. So, naturally, the question that surfaces is "...and now what?"

Presently, I don't see the risk-reward as being overly compelling, but I will keep a close eye on upcoming developments. In short, I'm neutral on this stock.

Firefly Aerospace's Near-Term Prospects

Firefly builds rockets and lunar landers. Its customers are mostly government and defense agencies, plus commercial entities, who need reliable and lower-cost ways to launch things and move around in space.

The idea being that Firefly provides a little of everything to get missions done while being cheaper than legacy aerospace approaches.

Plus, what truly drives the bull case is that Firefly already holds a $1.3 billion backlog. Not to mention that Firefly is one of the very few companies that has actually landed on the Moon and delivered payloads successfully (page 82).

There's no doubt that its pipeline is expanding, and the story is one of being in the right sector, with enticing prospects, but as an investor, I am not fully sold on its fundamentals, which we delve into next.

Revenue Growth Rates Are Challenging to Predict

After spending a considerable amount of time on the company's S-1 filing (IPO registration file), I have not been able to reconstruct what Firefly's Q4 2024 revenues reached.

As a consequence, even though Firefly guides for its full year 2025, it's difficult to ascertain what Q4 2025 revenue growth rates are likely to be, as we are not given their Q4 2024 to compare against. At least not yet. This detail will inevitably be provided when Firefly reports its Q4 2025 results in the coming weeks.

Why is this critical for investors? For two reasons.

Firstly, as you can see from the graphic above, Firefly's revenues are all over the place, depending on whether there's been a successful launch or not.

Case in point, Q1 2025 saw its revenues increase dramatically by more than 550% y/y, only for the subsequent quarter to report a negative 26% y/y revenue growth rate.

Without some sort of steady growth rates, this makes the second aspect close to impossible. How can we answer this question: what sort of revenue growth rates can we earnestly expect to see in 2026 and 2027?

But do we need to have this sort of visibility? Well, the issue is that if you are being asked to pay a large premium for a growth stock, you would generally demand some level of visibility. Not to mention the expectation of steady and strong growth, both of which Firefly fails to deliver.

With that in mind, let's dig further into its premium valuation.

FLY Stock Valuation -- +30x 2025 Revenues

As an Inflection investor, before I get stuck into a company's valuation, I find it of critical importance to know whether a company's balance sheet works for the bull thesis or against it.

After deploying $300 million of cash plus $555 million of equity to acquire SciTec, Firefly is now likely to hold less than $600 million of cash and marketable securities.

Superficially, this looks like a strong balance sheet, particularly since Firefly doesn't carry much debt.

But the issue is further confounded as Firefly is far from breaking even on its free cash flow line.

But before we address that issue, consider this: Firefly's management team doesn't carry much skin in the game here. In fact, as you can see in the SEC filing below, the majority of the stock is held by AE Industrial, a private equity firm.

This tells me two things. As an experienced investor, you'll know that no matter how passionate the management team is about their prospects, there's a massive gulf between being passionate about a business and running a business for profit.

On top of that, I believe that AE Industrial, as a private equity group, is most likely very eager to cash in on Firefly's profitable IPO to deploy their capital into new private equity operations. This is likely to create a meaningful overhang on the stock, as more than a third of the stock could be sold into the market in the near term.

And now, onto the real crux of the thesis. As you can see from the table that follows, the business is far from breaking even on its free cash flow line.

This tells me that in 2025, Firefly is likely to burn at least $200 million of free cash flow, as well as at least a further $150 million or more in 2026.

Since Firefly's balance sheet will have probably started 2026 with less than $600 million, this will mean that in 2026, Firefly will need to raise a minimum of $500 million in cash.

Or to put this more concretely, Firefly will probably dilute shareholders by a minimum of 10% in 2026. And that, I believe, is only a stepping stone before further dilution in 2027.

The Bottom Line

In conclusion, I do see a credible bull case forming over time, primarily driven by a $1.3 billion backlog, plus exposure to long-duration contracts.

What's more, if revenues begin to smooth out, its operating leverage could start to look powerful.

That being said, at over 30x 2025 revenues, together with highly volatile growth and demonstrably leaking free cash flow, I find myself on the sidelines for now.

Additionally, as noted in the analysis, the stock is likely facing substantial shareholder dilution in 2025 in an effort to shore up its balance sheet.

This is a stock I’ll keep firmly on my watchlist, but for now, I'm staying on the sidelines.
